title: Slicing cut
subtitle: Drawing the knife edge at an angle across end grain and tough fibres
excerpt: The slicing cut draws the knife edge diagonally across the wood rather than pushing it straight in. On end grain, difficult cross-grain faces, and the hardwood sections of a spoon or treen piece where a push cut simply skids, the slicing cut reduces resistance and produces a cleaner surface. This technique covers the grip, the angle, when to use it, and how it differs from a draw cut.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the diagonal-shearing cut principle applied to hand-tool woodwork. Early-twentieth-century Swedish slöjd manuals for the slicing-cut application on end grain.

content.text: The
slicing cut

works like a sharp kitchen knife on bread. Push the blade straight down and it crushes the loaf. Draw it at an angle and it slices cleanly. The same idea applies in wood. Use the slicing cut on end grain, hard cross-grain sections, the chamfer at the tip of a spoon handle, and anywhere a straight
push cut
would need too much force.
What it does

The blade moves across the wood in two directions simultaneously: forward into the cut and laterally along its own edge. This combined motion reduces the force required to penetrate the grain. On end grain, which is the hardest face to cut cleanly with a knife, the slicing motion splits the fibres along their length rather than crushing them across, producing a cleaner surface.
When to use it

On end grain: the rounded tip of a spoon handle, the chamfered end of a spurtle, the foot of a treen piece. On cross-grain faces where the push cut skids rather than biting. On dense seasoned hardwoods (cherry, oak, beech) where a push cut needs excessive pressure to start. The slicing cut is not a replacement for the push cut on long-grain shaping; use it only where the push cut fails.
What you need

A sloyd knife with a sharp edge; the slicing cut concentrates load on the very tip of the edge and requires keenness throughout the full length of the blade. Strop the edge before using the slicing cut on hard grain.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Set the blade angle

Hold the knife so the blade makes a thirty-to-forty-five-degree angle to the direction of travel rather than pointing straight ahead. The cutting edge faces the wood surface; the heel of the blade leads slightly. This orientation means the edge does not strike the grain perpendicular but at an angle.
Step 2: Execute the cut

Push the blade into the wood at the starting point and simultaneously draw it sideways along its own length while advancing it forward. The motion is a combination of push and draw: the forward component drives the blade into the cut, the lateral component sheers the fibres rather than crushing them. On end grain, the cut should start with the blade heel and finish with the blade tip engaged at the far end of the cut.
Step 3: Adjust depth with wrist, not forearm

The depth of a slicing cut is controlled by wrist rotation: rotating the wrist to drop the blade edge slightly deeper increases the cut depth; rotating it back reduces it. For fine work on end grain, keep the cut very shallow. A deep slicing cut on end grain removes material in a controlled way but is difficult to reverse; take several light passes rather than one deep one.
What it looks like when it is right

On end grain, a clean slicing cut leaves a smooth, almost polished surface: the fibres are split along their length and the cut face reflects light without raised or torn fibres. On long grain, the slicing cut leaves a surface that looks exactly like a push cut but required less force to achieve.
Common mistakes

Using the slicing cut as the default rather than the push cut: the slicing motion is less intuitive for beginners and can become an avoidance technique. The push cut is the faster and more controlled method on long grain.
Blade angle too large (more than fifty degrees): the cut becomes a draw cut rather than a slicing cut and removes material unpredictably. Keep the angle at thirty to forty-five degrees.
Dull blade: slicing cuts on end grain require more edge keenness than any other cut. A dull blade crushes rather than splits the end-grain fibres and produces a fuzzy surface regardless of technique.
